description: Virtual science lab lesson plan for a single student using the My Solar System Lab Simulation app, designed as a case-study investigation aligned to NGSS HS-ESS1-2A. Students investigate how mass, distance, speed, and gravitational interaction shape orbital motion and Earth’s place in the solar system through four inquiry-based lab parts.

learningGoals.bigIdea: Gravity and motion work together to determine the paths of planets, helping explain Earth’s place in the solar system.
objectives: Investigate how gravitational attraction between two bodies affects orbital motion in a Sun-planet system.
Analyze how changing distance influences orbital speed, orbit size, and orbital period.
Determine how initial speed changes can produce stable or unstable orbital paths.
Use simulation evidence to explain why Earth’s orbit is stable and how it compares with other possible planetary configurations.
successCriteria: I can describe how gravity and motion interact to create an orbit.
I can use simulation data to explain how distance affects orbital speed and period.
I can predict and justify how changing speed alters the path of a planet.
I can support a claim about Earth’s orbit using data from the simulation.

labParts.title: Case Study 1: Why Does the Planet Keep Missing the Sun?
setup: Open the simulation and select the Sun-Planet system from the body selector.
Set Body 1 to a large mass and Body 2 to a smaller mass using the mass sliders so the system resembles a star-planet pair.
Turn on Path and Center of Mass; keep Grid and Measuring Tape off for the first run.
Set speed to Normal and press Play.
Observe the orbit for at least one full cycle, then pause and reset if needed.
Use the zoom control only if the orbit extends beyond the screen.
observations: What shape does the planet’s path take around the star, and how does that shape help explain why the planet does not crash into the Sun?
Where is the center of mass located relative to the two bodies, and what does that suggest about which object exerts the stronger gravitational influence?
How does the distance between the two bodies change during the orbit, and what does that imply about gravitational attraction over time?
evidence: Collect and save a CSV file with columns for time, body 1 mass, body 2 mass, distance between bodies, and center of mass position for at least one complete orbit.
predictions: If the smaller body were moved farther from the larger body, what would you predict would happen to the orbit size and orbital period?
If the larger body’s mass were reduced, what would you predict would happen to the center of mass and the orbit shape?

labParts.title: Case Study 2: What Happens When Distance Changes?
setup: Clear the simulation and keep the Sun-Planet system selected.
Set the masses to the same values used in Part 1 so mass is controlled.
Turn on Path, Measuring Tape, and Speed (km/s); leave Velocity and Gravity Force off for now.
Use the measuring tape to estimate the starting distance between the two bodies.
Run the simulation at Normal speed, then repeat with the planet placed at a larger starting distance from the Sun.
Record results for both trials.
observations: How does the orbital path change when the starting distance from the Sun increases?
What happens to the planet’s speed when it is farther from the Sun compared with when it is closer?
How does the time required to complete one orbit change as distance increases?
evidence: Collect and save a CSV file with columns for trial, starting distance, orbital speed, and orbital period for at least two different distances.
predictions: If the planet were placed even farther from the Sun, what would you predict about its speed and orbital period?
If the planet were placed closer to the Sun, what would you predict about the strength of gravity and the tightness of the orbit?

labParts.title: Case Study 3: Can Speed Make a Planet Escape?
setup: Reset the simulation and keep the Sun-Planet system selected.
Use the same masses as in Part 1 and Part 2.
Turn on Path, Velocity, and Speed (km/s); optionally turn on Gravity Force for comparison.
Start with the planet at a moderate distance from the Sun.
Run three trials by changing the initial speed setting or adjusting the motion conditions available in the app, keeping mass and distance constant.
Compare a slower-than-orbital-speed trial, a near-stable-orbital-speed trial, and a faster-than-stable trial.
observations: What happens to the path when the planet’s speed is too low compared with the stable orbital case?
What happens to the path when the planet’s speed is too high compared with the stable orbital case?
How do the velocity and gravity force indicators help explain whether the planet stays in orbit, spirals inward, or escapes?
evidence: Collect and save a CSV file with columns for trial, initial speed, path type, and outcome of the motion (stable orbit, inward spiral, or escape).
predictions: If the planet’s speed were increased slightly above the stable value, what would you predict about the orbit shape?
If the planet’s speed were decreased slightly below the stable value, what would you predict about the planet’s future path?

labParts.title: Case Study 4: Which Combination Best Explains Earth’s Orbit?
setup: Reset the simulation and select the Sun-Planet system.
Set the Sun to a much larger mass than the planet, using the same general scale as earlier trials.
Turn on Path, Center of Mass, Speed (km/s), Velocity, and Gravity Force.
Use the zoom and measuring tape as needed to compare orbit size and distance.
Run the simulation with a configuration that best resembles Earth’s orbit, then compare it with one altered variable at a time: mass, distance, or speed.
Decide which combination produces the most stable, Earth-like orbit.
observations: Which combination of mass, distance, and speed produces the most stable orbit, and why does it seem most Earth-like?
How does the center of mass behave in the Earth-like configuration compared with the altered configurations?
What evidence from the path, speed, and force indicators suggests that gravity and motion are balanced in the best model?
evidence: Collect and save a CSV file with columns for configuration, body 1 mass, body 2 mass, starting distance, speed, orbital stability, and orbit type for all tested setups.
predictions: If Earth were much closer to the Sun, what would you predict about its orbital speed and climate implications?
If Earth were much farther from the Sun, what would you predict about its orbital period and the amount of solar energy received?

description: Virtual science lab lesson plan for a single student using the Natural Selection Lab Simulation app to investigate how environmental conditions, variation, and selection pressures change trait frequencies over generations through case-study-based scientific inquiry.

learningGoals.bigIdea: Natural selection changes populations over time because individuals with traits better suited to their environment are more likely to survive and reproduce.
objectives: Identify how environmental conditions influence survival and reproduction in a simulated rabbit population.
Analyze how changes in habitat, predators, and food supply alter trait frequencies across generations.
Investigate how mutation creates variation that can be acted on by natural selection.
Construct an evidence-based explanation of natural selection using simulation data and observed patterns.
successCriteria: I can describe how a specific environmental condition affects which rabbits survive.
I can compare trait data across generations and identify patterns of change.
I can explain how mutation contributes to variation in a population.
I can use CSV data from the simulation to support a claim about natural selection.

labParts.title: Case Study 1: Desert Survival and the First Clues of Natural Selection
setup: Open the Natural Selection Lab Simulation app and select the Introduction to Natural Selection tab.
Set habitat to Desert.
Set wolves to Few.
Set food supply to Normal.
Set mutation rate to 10%.
Record the starting population counts and the visible rabbit trait patterns before advancing generations.
Run the simulation for 3 generations using the Next Generation control, pausing after each generation to inspect the population and trait graphs.
observations: What rabbit traits seem most common at the start of the simulation, and how might those traits help or hinder survival in a desert habitat?
What changes do you notice in the population size and trait distribution after each generation?
Which rabbits appear more likely to survive in the desert, and what evidence from the simulation supports that idea?
evidence: Collect and save a CSV file named part1_desert_baseline.csv with columns for generation, population size, average speed, average camouflage, average size, habitat, wolves, food supply, and mutation rate.
predictions: If the simulation continues for more generations in the desert, which trait do you predict will become more common, and why?
How do you predict the rabbit population will change if the environment stays the same but the generations continue?

labParts.title: Case Study 2: Environmental Change as a Selection Pressure
setup: Keep the same simulation and switch the habitat from Desert to Forest.
Change wolves to Many.
Change food supply to Scarce.
Keep mutation rate at 10% for a controlled comparison.
Run the simulation for 3 additional generations, recording changes after each generation.
Compare the new results to the desert baseline and note which traits increase or decrease in frequency.
observations: How does changing the habitat from desert to forest affect which rabbits are easier or harder to spot?
What happens to the population when predators increase and food becomes scarce?
Which trait changes seem linked to survival under the new environmental conditions?
evidence: Collect and save a CSV file named part2_environmental_change.csv with columns for generation, population size, average speed, average camouflage, average size, habitat, wolves, food supply, and mutation rate.
predictions: If the forest conditions continue, which rabbit trait do you predict will be favored by natural selection?
What do you predict will happen to the population if wolves remain many and food remains scarce for several more generations?

labParts.title: Case Study 3: Mutation, Variation, and the Source of New Traits
setup: Reset or continue with a fresh trial in the simulation to isolate the effect of mutation.
Set habitat to Arctic.
Set wolves to Some.
Set food supply to Normal.
Increase mutation rate to High variation if available, or move the slider to the highest setting shown.
Run the simulation for 3 generations and watch for new trait combinations or shifts in trait averages.
Compare the trait graph and population graph to earlier trials.
observations: What new trait differences appear when mutation rate is increased?
How does higher variation affect the range of traits in the rabbit population?
Do any traits seem to give certain rabbits a better chance of surviving in the arctic habitat?
evidence: Collect and save a CSV file named part3_mutation_variation.csv with columns for generation, population size, average speed, average camouflage, average size, habitat, wolves, food supply, and mutation rate.
predictions: If mutation remains high, which new trait variation do you predict may become more common over time?
How do you predict increased variation will affect the population's ability to adapt to changing conditions?

labParts.title: Case Study 4: Explaining Natural Selection with Evidence
setup: Choose one habitat condition from the previous trials that produced the clearest trait shift.
Set wolves, food supply, and mutation rate to the same values used in that trial.
Run the simulation for 3 more generations to confirm the pattern.
Use the analysis graphs to compare population trends and trait averages across generations.
Prepare a short evidence-based explanation of how the environment influenced which rabbits survived and reproduced.
observations: What consistent pattern do you see between the environment and the traits that become more common?
How does the population graph support or challenge your explanation of natural selection?
What evidence shows that some rabbits were more successful at surviving and reproducing than others?
evidence: Collect and save a CSV file named part4_claim_evidence_reasoning.csv with columns for generation, population size, average speed, average camouflage, average size, habitat, wolves, food supply, and mutation rate.
predictions: Based on your evidence, what do you predict would happen if the same population were placed in a different habitat?
What do you predict would happen to trait frequencies if the selection pressure were removed?
